content:
8 min read
Just now

--

Forums are social economies, sticking code reviews on them doesn’t make them suddenly any less predatory
Stop putting your work online, no one will suddenly say this is the best way of doing this. It’s not only a issue with people who think they need recognition but also the entire idea that suddenly after releasing free things that the person who did so is somehow morally justified to ascertain who is better, then ultimately their capitalist mindset comes to surface.
It is not a simple attempt to share knowledge, but a deliberate attempt at sharing a advertisement style version of your personality. No one cares about your niche subject, and nothing can be done for it to be the alternative. Your work at this moment gets validated against the potential of the people who view the work itself, if the person who validates and uses your work is in some way knowledgable then the capitalistic arrogance takes over. Open source is dead because the crab theory not only pulls it toward slop dressed as solutions, but it creates a elitism of who is logically inferior.
Software development circles spend their time not fixing issues, but they actually spend their time preparing on how they can show off their work in the most consumer friendly advert-able way. Your style of speaking injects personality in a place where personality is valued against the product- regardless if it exists or not. Your entire brain chemistry revolves around impressing people who are fundamentally too selfish for impressions to land in a organic way. Take LLM’s for example, if you sit there and find a solution but the LLM says that it’s some breakthrough then instead of listening to a contextless prompt response, the rigorous training and validation needed becomes a question of can we do this AI? Neither answer solves the issue or brings you closer, its the illusion of work being put on your shoulder.
Peer based validation of turned any sort of constructive online sharing into a performance based mutation of the former self and open source is code word for cult of personality around the person who created the work in the first place. It invalidates real work in favour of being a pedestal for decision making because “they did it before”. The ingenuity doesn’t matter, your fixes don’t matter and the sharing mentality is a way for maintainers to not only get free work, but be able to profit in favour of the source project. There is a ideological gap, and it’s defined by the mannerisms, secrecy and perpetuity in distanced communications. It’s a incentive that makes free work be unrecognized and just another cog in the machine- no matter how influential or good it may be. Maintainers dress up to look like they are solving problems because they become stewards of a community that can be extracted on, and any sort of solution becomes a injection of collective doubt dressed as a issue request. End users don’t give a crap how you solve a problem, or if you ever did it in the first place. Any sort of semblance of intrigue beyond the initial information becomes a fundamental test advertisement potential. You’re not solving technology, you’re fixing your ego.
Egotism is the largest driving factor in how software communities behave, and this is extremely egalitarian in principle- but in practice it’s actually a competition of who can get their number up higher. It’s the discord moderation job of programming, in the sense that discord mods are extreme “online” versions of their personality, the people who maintain software are using the same structure while being ambivalent on the pedestal that it creates. If the system you created lets you dismiss the work proposed, then the work becomes a sorting hat with extra steps. Any programming knowledge put out has to be immediately contrasted with the history of contribution, it’s because programmers don’t like to have surprises.
Programming itself is a exercise in control over a machine, your work is technical and not actually creative or research bound. Systems are boilerplates with lego pieces and the communities that surround them supercharge this definition as being some “holy” one sided understanding which culminates in a stigmatized view of anyone outside of the immediate review circle. Your brain becomes rotten from the egotism that is provided, and conventional tracking softwares- even while used by thousands; foster this top down elitism very effectively. It’s a exercise in how the information is drained, without sustainability. The stigma is a direct self-serving ego pit that is based on performative contributions and extending their visibility to the maximum point. A simple solution is not accepted as the potential one, because there not is a check of conformance to the architectural community, and this itself makes software monolithic and ideological.
Take a good hard look at what the similarities are, there is the idea of a discord mod, the combination of controlling and often times a power-oriented role and specifically infamous for being a style of grooming people. Reality hits hard when you see that Open source is a grooming kit for once large scale contributors being repurposed as a mentality funnel. Your code is shit, it doesn’t matter how big or how many people use it. It’s inherently useless until you make it useful. Open source does not make things suddenly not shit, it makes them maintain the existing level of crap. This is the simplest way to explain how alienating and why open source communities look like big bad groups from the outside. Lead maintainers fall in to this harbouring for a cult of personality when they themselves are a frequent contributor- this same-y way of looking at it makes the entire community look like a stuck up members only club. Nothing gets solved, and over time “maintained” as a concept itself provides a tower style community where whomever is at the top of the tower is the “mind at the forefront”.
Open source projects that do not exhibit this are either too small and have their roots in a lead maintainer, or are structurally gated via a committee style power balance. If open source projects were really suffering from a bottom up problem of beginners being sloppy, then they themselves would setup a way for decoupling the newbies and the real canonical flow. Open source is not a environment where you can flourish, and it is especially not a environment where extended learning is permitted. Open source places itself at the frontier of computing knowledge by cutting the low hanging fruit out, and anything that isn’t submitted by a significant contributor becomes culled without consideration. This is a blatant admission of not only what doesn’t work, but also things that DO, Open source becomes a more strict and top down structure than a company itself. Open source breeds the mentality of being better one way or another, it hitches on someones expectation that the public part makes them be entitled to a position in a community where the voices themselves are regulated. In any other place, open source would be considered corruption from a long distanced view.
Open source becomes a version non improvement, and collapses each project itself as a social platform for “karma”. No new features appear without extreme time sinks, if the project was not done in the first place, it’s not in the position for Open source to fix it in the first place. It actually means that any drift from the original implementation becomes quantified as a larger impact than any sort of tricks or solutions.
You would think that when LLM’s arrive, your projects would iterate faster; this is doubly true for open source and the paradox is simple, if the community itself surrounding a open source program has longstanding contributions their work is quantified by the amount of fixes not by the merit and thought processes behind them. It’s a way of decoupling the user from the group, and therefore under this lens it’s seen that quick iteration is a attack, not a improvement. Any sort of automation is not contrasted against structure, but the identity of the person contributing becomes a ambiguous boogeyman in every single open source community. This is the gatekeeping but not only that, it shows ideological absolutism and specifically gives people who wouldn’t usually be intrusive more moral justification for taking time to search someones background up (very often with AI), specifically sort them in a way where the boogeyman analogy becomes reachable, and then the previous instances even if unrelated become a stigmatized way of projecting a mentality downward. It’s a oligarchical and patronizing gatekeeping attempt, not a functional one. This boogeyman reach is the sole and major pointing factor at how things can justify immorality. Psychoanalysis, physical shaming, decision and intellect ad hominem are extremely common when this connection is made. I’m here telling you that a bourgeois group is the antithesis of the ethos of what it means to be open source, and this is how casual relational mocking becomes a societal moral grandstanding.
Open source communities are overlaying dangerous rhetoric to justify insulting a non-conventional perspective. This makes the minimum requirement to be a part of the group be literally proof of absolutism in ideological alignment with others. Pretending that you are better than others by projecting your dislikes on to them is the very way racism, and bigotry spreads. The people who think that this logic cannot be applied on committees project their line drawn in sand, and this is where the exact system does not appear visible. People who are part of the “clique” are the gatekeepers, not the entire community as a whole- but the select few that commandeer a sense of moral or longstanding history. It is applicable at a 2 person level, not across the entire history of maintainers. Any gatekeeping will fundamentally be dressed as “needed” in different strengths depending on how deep the issue lands. It doesn’t need to be at a large scale coordinated level but it becomes a interpersonal hierarchy like in sims where each interaction becomes a internal bonus point to a “connection” which then lets people validate each other on attention rather than merit. It is not, and will never be a meritocracy, because at each level the social dynamics become a case of red herrings being placed as trip mines for the people that don’t know to mock or be happy with the specific topic. It breeds not only the top layer cult of personality but legitimately a hierarchical stack of them, which by this description becomes a information funnel.
Listen though, the entire world is more nuanced and pushing people in a box is how you get them to behave un-naturally. This is what breeds extremism, a person pressured to act differently will act as the worst thing they know to be and not be that worst thing in reality. This pressure makes any sort of counter argument look charged, and no matter what this becomes crash in respect. This culminates in a psychological armchair projection, a framework of rejection based on seeing a person as manic, and more importantly disregard their logic not from argument but from collective opinion whether this new person fits in. Performative indoctrination is visible at all levels, and this can be taken to any sort of public institutions as the core example. Government itself is open source, at least modern governments. The places that are closed are the people at the top who have to maintain, oligarchical power structure stands regardless.
By enforcing some sort of minimal labor, emotional or to build credentials before giving a unequal opportunity. The only way to stop social pressure is by anonymizing the telemetry and the contributions- and credentials should be standardized and not directly tracked for the best innovation. This requires the most effort, and retains the specific ability to add to the functi
